cards.text: The conditional operator {{c1::?:}} is the only {{c2::ternary}} operator in C++ (takes three operands). It's also called the {{c3::arithmetic if}} operator.
The conditional operator syntax is {{c1::condition ? expressionIfTrue : expressionIfFalse}}. If condition is true, {{c2::expressionIfTrue}} is evaluated and returned.

cards.question: What does this code output?
cards.explanation: x > 3 is true (5 > 3), so the conditional operator returns 10.

cards.question: Rewrite using the conditional operator:
cards.explanation: The conditional operator provides a concise way to assign one of two values based on a condition.

cards.front: When should you parenthesize the conditional operator?
cards.back: Always parenthesize the entire conditional operator when used in a compound expression. For readability, also parenthesize the condition if it contains operators.

cards.text: The conditional operator evaluates as an {{c1::expression}}, not a statement, so it can be used anywhere a value is needed (assignments, function arguments, etc.).

cards.question: What does this function return for num = 1?
cards.explanation: When num is 1, the condition (num == 1) is true, so it returns "apple".

cards.front: Can you use the conditional operator for type conversion?
cards.back: Yes. The conditional operator performs usual arithmetic conversions on the true and false expressions, so (true ? 1 : 2.5) returns double.

cards.question: What's a better way to write this?
cards.code: std::cout << (x < y ? (x < z ? x : z) : (y < z ? y : z));
cards.answer: Use a function or if-else statements. Nested ternaries are hard to read.
cards.explanation: While nested conditional operators work, they quickly become unreadable. Use if-else or a function for complex logic.

cards.text: The conditional operator only evaluates {{c1::one}} of the two expressions (either true or false branch), not both. This is called {{c2::short-circuit}} evaluation.

cards.concept: When to use the conditional operator vs if-else
cards.explanation:
Use the conditional operator for simple conditional assignments or return values. Use if-else for:
  • Complex conditions
  • Multiple statements in branches
  • Better readability
The conditional operator works best when assigning one of two values.
examples: int max = (a > b) ? a : b;  // Good use

cards.text: The ternary operator evaluates: if {{c1::condition}} is true, return {{c2::expressionIfTrue}}, else return {{c3::expressionIfFalse}}
